text:
Just had a great design review today with the Tesla AI5 chip design team! This is going to be an epic chip. 

And AI6 to follow has a shot at being the best by AI chip by far.

Switching from doing 2 chip architectures to 1 means all our silicon talent is focused on making 1 incredible chip. No-brainer in retrospect.

Please join the Tesla silicon team if you want to work on chips that save lives. Milliseconds matter.

sex offender registries in Tennessee and Texas list individuals with clear non-European ancestry as \"White\" or \"White Hispanic\" under race, despite their ethnicities being marked as \"Non-Hispanic,\" suggesting a potential misclassification that aligns with historical FBI Uniform Crime Report (UCR) practices, which until 2013 categorized 93% of Hispanics as \"White,\" inflating white crime statistics as noted in a 2025 Wikipedia update on race and crime.\n\n- This discrepancy may reflect systemic issues in crime data collection, supported by a 1995 study by Hagan and Peterson linking racial segregation and poverty to higher crime rates, implying that mislabeling could obscure socioeconomic factors driving crime rather than accurately reflecting racial demographics.\n\n- Evidence from a 2025 Fox News report on a D.C. police commander’s suspension for allegedly altering crime data suggests that such manipulations are not isolated, raising questions about the integrity of public safety statistics and their use in policy, especially when challenged by experts like Jim Agresti who argue for contextual analysis over cherry-picked figures."}]}, I’ve never been one to flaunt my allegiance in public. But this morning, I put on a Freedom shirt. We’ve made two stops. At one, I was given nasty looks. At the other, a man was super nice to me when he sat at the table behind us at a restaurant. When he saw my shirt, he went out of his way to be rude to me in front of my child. I’m beyond disgusted. Want a reason to hate me? I am a Constitutional Conservative. I am a Christian. And I am not going to be silent anymore. text:
@SemiAnalysis_ @BrentM_SpaceX 😉

Still small fry at 1GW. 

For serious power in the 100GW continuous range (~1/5 of USA average power consumption), it probably needs to be solar/battery powered. 

At 1TW+, solar/battery is the only realistic option.

Crack on with the day.

Things you’ll never do again if you buy a Tesla:

-Start the car 
-Turn the car off
-Unlock the car
-Lock the car
-Turn on the headlights
-Turn off the headlights 
-Carry a car key 
-Put the car in drive or reverse
-Put the car in park 
-Get an oil change
-Interface with a gas

text: It just works. You can summon your Tesla from your phone.
embedded_text: Went to the mall today and in typical form failed to note where I parked. Instead of looking for it, I tried something crazy and summoned the car from the Tesla app. 30 seconds later, the empty car emerged from one of the lanes and pulled up in front of me. We live in the future.

text:
Better to live life erring on the side of being optimistic and wrong than pessimistic and right!

Be realistic, but, as Monty Python would say, always look on the bright side of life!
embedded_text: The only time more exciting to be alive than today is tomorrow.

text:
I love trains, but the future is individualized mass transit with autonomous electric vehicles taking you precisely to your destination. 

Tunnels are needed to address the fundamental choke points in cities. You can’t have 3D buildings and expect a 2D transport system to be effective! Multiple layers of tunnels enable transport to be 3D too.

The fastest way to travel long distances city center to city center, short of new physics, is cars/pods in vacuum tunnels.

text: Anyone who celebrates murder is evil and should be utterly condemned
embedded_text:
There’s a new political divide forming:

If you celebrate when an innocent person whose political views differ from you is assassinated you’re gone.

Don’t care what that person’s political persuasion is or how strongly you disagree with them.

Cant think of one counterexample.

After reviewing the channels", "id_str": "1972263703149002980", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 2531, "reply_count": 4615, "retweet_count": 24462, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "16477634"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjE5NzIyNjM3MDI5NzY5NTg0NjQ=", "text": "🇲🇩 About a year ago, while I was stuck in Paris, the French intelligence services reached out to me through an intermediary, asking me to help the Moldovan government censor certain Telegram channels ahead of the presidential elections in Moldova. After reviewing the channels flagged by French (and Moldovan) authorities, we identified a few that clearly violated our rules and removed them. The intermediary then informed me that, in exchange for this cooperation, French intelligence would “say good things” about me to the judge who had ordered my arrest in August last year. This was unacceptable on several levels. If the agency did in fact approach the judge — it constituted an attempt to interfere in the judicial process. If it did not, and merely claimed to have done so, then it was exploiting my legal situation in France to influence political developments in Eastern Europe — a pattern we have also observed in Romania 🇷🇴 Shortly thereafter, the Telegram team received a second list of so-called “problematic” Moldovan channels. Unlike the first, nearly all of these channels were legitimate and fully compliant with our rules. Their only commonality was that they voiced political positions disliked by the French and Moldovan governments. We refused to act on this request. Telegram is committed to freedom of speech and will not remove content for political reasons. I will continue to expose every attempt to pressure Telegram into censoring our platform. {"__typename": "Tweet", "card": {"legacy": {"binding_values": [{"key": "unified_card", "value": {"string_value": "{\"component_objects\":{\"details_1\":{\"type\":\"grok_share\",\"data\":{\"profile_user\":{\"id_str\":\"44196397\",\"profile_image_url_https\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/2035314704307081216\\/71U1ftM3.jpg\",\"name\":\"Elon Musk\",\"screen_name\":\"elonmusk\",\"highlighted_label\":{\"description\":\"X\",\"badge\":{\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1955359038532653056\\/OSHY3ewP_bigger.jpg\"},\"url\":{\"urlType\":\"DeepLink\",\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/twitter.com\\/X\"},\"userLabelType\":\"BusinessLabel\",\"userLabelDisplayType\":\"Badge\"},\"verified\":false,\"is_blue_verified\":true},\"grok_user\":{\"id_str\":\"1720665183188922368\",\"profile_image_url_https\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1893219113717342208\\/Vgg2hEPa.jpg\",\"name\":\"Grok\",\"screen_name\":\"grok\",\"highlighted_label\":{\"description\":\"xAI\",\"badge\":{\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1769430779845611520\\/lIgjSJGU_bigger.jpg\"},\"url\":{\"urlType\":\"DeepLink\",\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/twitter.com\\/xai\"},\"userLabelType\":\"BusinessLabel\",\"userLabelDisplayType\":\"Badge\"},\"verified\":false,\"is_blue_verified\":true,\"verified_type\":\"Business\"},\"id\":\"5329fvvy5wTv8elCQs4d7ut2v\",\"conversation_preview\":[{\"message\":\"Analyze why this works\",\"sender\":\"USER\",\"grokMode\":\"NORMAL\",\"mediaUrls\":[]},{\"message\":\"Understanding the \\\"Lucky Training Run\\\" in the GLU Paper\\nThe X post you shared highlights a candid moment from the 2020 arXiv paper \\\"GLU Variants Improve Transformer\\\" (arXiv:2002.05202v1), where the authors\\u2014Sharan Narang, Hyung Won Chung, and others\\u2014test several Gated Linear Unit (GLU) variants as alternatives to standard activations like ReLU or GELU in Transformer feed-forward layers. Their key finding? Variants like GEGLU (GELU-gated) and SwiGLU (Swish-gated) often outperformed baselines on pre-training perplexity and downstream tasks (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE), but they cheekily attribute this to \\\"divine benevolence\\\" rather than deep insight, implying a single \\\"lucky\\\" training run drove the headline results. This isn't hyperbole\\u2014it's a nod to the high inter-run variability they observed, with standard deviations across seeds sometimes rivaling the mean improvements.\\nSo, why does this \\\"luck\\\" happen? Why can a random seed turn a middling architecture tweak into a seeming breakthrough? Below, I'll break it down step-by-step, drawing on the paper's context and broader deep learning principles. The short answer: Deep learning's stochastic nature\\u2014especially in massive models like Transformers\\u2014means tiny random variations can cascade into big performance swings due to chaotic optimization dynamics.\\nRandom Weight Initialization Sets the Optimization Trajectory\\n   - At the start of training, neural network weights (millions to billions in Transformers) are initialized randomly, often using Xavier\\/Glorot or He methods scaled by a seed-controlled random number generator. This seed dictates the exact starting point in the model's parameter space.\\n   - Deep learning loss landscapes are highly non-convex: a rugged, high-dimensional terrain with countless local minima. Different initializations lead to divergent gradient descent paths. One seed might guide the model toward a \\\"sharp\\\" minimum (good training but poor generalization), while another hits a \\\"flat\\\" one (better overall performance).\\n   - In the GLU paper, they pre-trained T5-like models (220M parameters) on the C4 dataset for ~500k steps. With no dropout during pre-training and an Adafactor optimizer, small init differences could amplify quickly. Their Table 1 shows variability across just four short runs (65k steps): e.g., GEGLU perplexity ranged from ~2.05 to ~2.12 (std ~0.03), enough to flip \\\"best variant\\\" rankings.\\n   - Broader evidence: Studies like Raffel et al. (the T5 paper, arXiv:1910.10683) report similar seed-induced std devs of 0.5\\u20131% on GLUE tasks, showing Transformers are particularly sensitive due to their scale and depth.\\nData Shuffling and Mini-Batch Stochasticity Amplify Differences\\n   - Training data is shuffled per epoch using the same seed, affecting mini-batch compositions. A \\\"lucky\\\" seed might sequence examples in a way that smooths gradients early on, avoiding early plateaus or exploding gradients.\\n   - In Transformers, self-attention layers make the model hypersensitive to input order\\u2014subtle batch variations can shift attention patterns, altering learned representations. For GLU variants, which gate activations (e.g., GEGLU = GELU(x) * linear(y)), this interacts with the gating mechanism: a favorable early shuffle might better align the gate's \\\"focus\\\" on salient features, boosting perplexity on denoising tasks.\\n   - Example from practice: In data splitting (train\\/val\\/test), seed changes can skew subset statistics (e.g., class balance or token distributions in C4), leading to 5\\u201310% accuracy swings in classification. The GLU experiments used fixed splits but still saw run-to-run noise from shuffling.\\nModel Sensitivity and Overfitting in High-Dimensional Spaces\\n   - Large models like T5 are prone to overfitting, where performance hinges on noise-fitting rather than true signal. If the dataset is noisy or limited (C4 is massive but web-scraped), a lucky seed might \\\"accidentally\\\" regularize better via init noise.\\n   - GLU variants add gating, which can act like implicit sparsity\\u2014 but this benefits some inits more than others. The paper notes parameter parity (e.g., shrinking hidden size from 3072 to 2048 for GLUs), but doesn't explore why GEGLU\\/SwiGLU shine: likely because their smoother activations (GELU\\/Swish) pair well with certain random starts, avoiding vanishing gradients.\\n   - Quantitatively: Variability often scales with model size; in smaller nets, it's \\u003c1%, but in Transformers, it can hit 2\\u20135% on benchmarks. This is why papers now report means\\/stds over 3\\u20135 seeds.\\nWhy Transformers (and This Paper) Are Especially Prone\\n   - Transformers lack inductive biases like CNN locality, relying purely on learned attention\\u2014making them \\\"chaotic\\\" to init perturbations. The GLU paper's setup (no dropout, long training) exacerbates this; adding noise (e.g., dropout) can stabilize but wasn't used here for \\\"better results.\\\"\\n   - The \\\"divine benevolence\\\" quip underscores a field-wide issue: Many breakthroughs (e.g., early BERT tweaks) stem from unreported lucky runs, not rigor. Recent work quantifies this: Seed changes can shift fine-tuned Transformer outcomes by 1\\u20133 points on GLUE, enough to claim SOTA.\\nImplications and Fixes\\nThis variability isn't a bug\\u2014it's deep learning's feature, enabling exploration of the loss landscape. But it erodes reproducibility: The GLU paper's best run might not replicate, as later confirmations (e.g., in PaLM or GPT scaling laws) show GLU-like gates help consistently on average. To mitigate:\\nRun 5\\u201310 seeds and report stats.\\nUse seed-agnostic init (e.g., deterministic orthogonal).\\nStabilize via larger batches or SWA (stochastic weight averaging).\\nIn essence, the \\\"why it works\\\" is probabilistic serendipity: The right seed nudges the optimizer into a sweet spot where GLU gating unlocks better feature interactions. It's a reminder that ML progress often rides on chaos, not just clever math. {"items": [{"media_urls": [], "message": "Analyze why this works"}, {"deepsearch_headers": [{"steps": [{}, {}]}, {"steps": [{}]}], "media_urls": [], "message": "### Understanding the \"Lucky Training Run\" in the GLU Paper\n\nThe X post you shared highlights a candid moment from the 2020 arXiv paper \"GLU Variants Improve Transformer\" (arXiv:2002.05202v1), where the authors—Sharan Narang, Hyung Won Chung, and others—test several Gated Linear Unit (GLU) variants as alternatives to standard activations like ReLU or GELU in Transformer feed-forward layers. Their key finding? Variants like GEGLU (GELU-gated) and SwiGLU (Swish-gated) often outperformed baselines on pre-training perplexity and downstream tasks (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE), but they cheekily attribute this to \"divine benevolence\" rather than deep insight, implying a single \"lucky\" training run drove the headline results. This isn't hyperbole—it's a nod to the high inter-run variability they observed, with standard deviations across seeds sometimes rivaling the mean improvements.\n\nSo, why does this \"luck\" happen? Why can a random seed turn a middling architecture tweak into a seeming breakthrough? Below, I'll break it down step-by-step, drawing on the paper's context and broader deep learning principles. The short answer: Deep learning's stochastic nature—especially in massive models like Transformers—means tiny random variations can cascade into big performance swings due to chaotic optimization dynamics.\n\n#### 1. **Random Weight Initialization Sets the Optimization Trajectory**\n   - At the start of training, neural network weights (millions to billions in Transformers) are initialized randomly, often using Xavier/Glorot or He methods scaled by a seed-controlled random number generator. This seed dictates the exact starting point in the model's parameter space.\n   - Deep learning loss landscapes are highly non-convex: a rugged, high-dimensional terrain with countless local minima. Different initializations lead to divergent gradient descent paths. One seed might guide the model toward a \"sharp\" minimum (good training but poor generalization), while another hits a \"flat\" one (better overall performance).\n   - In the GLU paper, they pre-trained T5-like models (220M parameters) on the C4 dataset for ~500k steps. With no dropout during pre-training and an Adafactor optimizer, small init differences could amplify quickly. Their Table 1 shows variability across just four short runs (65k steps): e.g., GEGLU perplexity ranged from ~2.05 to ~2.12 (std ~0.03), enough to flip \"best variant\" rankings.\n   - Broader evidence: Studies like Raffel et al. (the T5 paper, arXiv:1910.10683) report similar seed-induced std devs of 0.5–1% on GLUE tasks, showing Transformers are particularly sensitive due to their scale and depth.\n\n#### 2. **Data Shuffling and Mini-Batch Stochasticity Amplify Differences**\n   - Training data is shuffled per epoch using the same seed, affecting mini-batch compositions. A \"lucky\" seed might sequence examples in a way that smooths gradients early on, avoiding early plateaus or exploding gradients.\n   - In Transformers, self-attention layers make the model hypersensitive to input order—subtle batch variations can shift attention patterns, altering learned representations. For GLU variants, which gate activations (e.g., GEGLU = GELU(x) * linear(y)), this interacts with the gating mechanism: a favorable early shuffle might better align the gate's \"focus\" on salient features, boosting perplexity on denoising tasks.\n   - Example from practice: In data splitting (train/val/test), seed changes can skew subset statistics (e.g., class balance or token distributions in C4), leading to 5–10% accuracy swings in classification. The GLU experiments used fixed splits but still saw run-to-run noise from shuffling.\n\n#### 3. **Model Sensitivity and Overfitting in High-Dimensional Spaces**\n   - Large models like T5 are prone to overfitting, where performance hinges on noise-fitting rather than true signal. If the dataset is noisy or limited (C4 is massive but web-scraped), a lucky seed might \"accidentally\" regularize better via init noise.\n   - GLU variants add gating, which can act like implicit sparsity— but this benefits some inits more than others. The paper notes parameter parity (e.g., shrinking hidden size from 3072 to 2048 for GLUs), but doesn't explore why GEGLU/SwiGLU shine: likely because their smoother activations (GELU/Swish) pair well with certain random starts, avoiding vanishing gradients.\n   - Quantitatively: Variability often scales with model size; in smaller nets, it's <1%, but in Transformers, it can hit 2–5% on benchmarks. This is why papers now report means/stds over 3–5 seeds.\n\n#### 4. **Why Transformers (and This Paper) Are Especially Prone**\n   - Transformers lack inductive biases like CNN locality, relying purely on learned attention—making them \"chaotic\" to init perturbations. The GLU paper's setup (no dropout, long training) exacerbates this; adding noise (e.g., dropout) can stabilize but wasn't used here for \"better results.\"\n   - The \"divine benevolence\" quip underscores a field-wide issue: Many breakthroughs (e.g., early BERT tweaks) stem from unreported lucky runs, not rigor. Recent work quantifies this: Seed changes can shift fine-tuned Transformer outcomes by 1–3 points on GLUE, enough to claim SOTA.\n\n#### Implications and Fixes\nThis variability isn't a bug—it's deep learning's feature, enabling exploration of the loss landscape. But it erodes reproducibility: The GLU paper's best run might not replicate, as later confirmations (e.g., in PaLM or GPT scaling laws) show GLU-like gates help consistently *on average*. To mitigate:\n- Run 5–10 seeds and report stats.\n- Use seed-agnostic init (e.g., deterministic orthogonal).\n- Stabilize via larger batches or SWA (stochastic weight averaging).\n\nIn essence, the \"why it works\" is probabilistic serendipity: The right seed nudges the optimizer into a sweet spot where GLU gating unlocks better feature interactions. It's a reminder that ML progress often rides on chaos, not just clever math. text:
Designing dexterous hands for robots is a very hard problem. 

Then figuring out how to manufacture them at scale is 100 times harder.
embedded_text:
This is the chaos produced by a triple pendulum with just 3 degrees of freedom.

Your hand has 27: 4 per finger + 5 for the thumb + 6 for the wrist

Building a robot to conduct this orchestra of joints and tendons to pick up a coin is ... non-trivial. https://t.co/PGOUeYTOZd

4568}]}, "text": "Here's how to vote in @Tesla's shareholder meeting: For $TSLA shareholders who use Fidelity: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). The email may come from one of several different addresses. Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “Tesla”, “https://t.co/pLKGQmwKJ4.email@shareholderdocs.fidelity.com”, “e-notification@edocs.mybrokerageinfo.com”, or “id@proxyvote.com”. Open the email and follow described instructions to vote. Robinhood: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “noreply@robinhood.com Tesla”. Open the email and click VOTE. Interactive Brokers: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “interactivebrokers@proxydocs.com Tesla”. Open the email and follow described instructions. Charles Schwab: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “id@proxyvote.com Tesla”. Additionally, you can log into your brokerage account, use the keywords ‘proxy events’ in the search bar and navigate to proxy events. Alternatively, search for a 16-digit control number in your postal mail. Visit https://t.co/nc7qLyN6sB and submit your 16-digit control number to vote. Morgan Stanley/E*Trade: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “id@proxyvote.com Tesla”. Open the email and follow described instructions. Merrill Lynch: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “id@proxyvote.com Tesla”. The email may come from one of several different addresses so please search your inbox for “Tesla” as well. Open the email and follow described instructions. Vanguard Brokerage: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: “id@proxyvote.com Tesla”. Open the email and follow described instructions. J.P. Morgan: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Search for an email received in 2025 using the following keywords: "id@proxyvote.com Tesla" Open the email and follow described instructions. If you cannot locate this email, contact JPM support at 888-938-4409 to learn how to vote. All Other Brokerages: • To cast your vote online, you’ll need to search for a specific email in your inbox, which may be in your spam folder (same email address that is associated with your broker account). Try searching for each of these three terms to see if you can locate an email from your broker sent in 2025: “id@proxyvote.com Tesla” “@proxydocs.com Tesla” “@saytechnologies.com Tesla” If you find an email, open it and follow described instructions. International $TSLA holders: Some brokers outside of the U.S. don’t allow retail shareholders to vote. If you have not received any voting instructions, please contact your broker to confirm if they provide for proxy voting and, if so, to request such instructions. If you have any additional questions regarding how to vote, please contact Tesla's proxy solicitor, Innisfree M&A Incorporated, at (877) 717-3936 (from the United States or Canada) or +1 (412) 232-3651 (from other locations). Sometimes, when you don't participate in proxy voting, brokers may exercise their discretion to cast votes on your behalf, so be proactive! General info: If you cannot locate any emails, try voting by mail, phone or QR code. If you have not received any instructions whether via email or by mail, contact your broker as soon as possible to request such instructions. More info here: • Vote by phone: https://t.co/rvM5TD4hPX • Vote by QR code: https://t.co/pDlyEiInUS • Vote by mail: https://t.co/XPsgoMZslH Every vote counts, whether you own one share or many. Make your voices heard. It only takes a few minutes. Reminder: You must have been a Tesla shareholder as of the September 15th record date to be able to vote. Tesla's shareholder meeting will take place on November 6, 2025 in Austin, Texas. Voting is now open, so go vote!"}}}, "rest_id": "1970263178266480888", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "29636495", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1970345771066093622", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28769670", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}
